greens_spoon_feeding.primary_goal: Maintain consistent growth, density, and recovery on intensively managed putting greens without large flushes.
core_principles: Favor frequent light nitrogen inputs over infrequent heavy shots during stress periods.
Adjust nitrogen to clipping yield, traffic, and season rather than using the same rate year-round.
Pair fertility decisions with topdressing, moisture management, and growth regulation.

benchmark_ranges.light_growth_periods: 0.05-0.1 lb N/1000 sq ft per application
benchmark_ranges.active_recovery_periods: 0.1-0.125 lb N/1000 sq ft per application
benchmark_ranges.interval: Every 7-14 days depending on growth demand

cautions: Avoid pushing lush growth ahead of heat, humidity, or anthracnose pressure.
Do not interpret color alone as proof of adequate nutrition.
Monitor potassium and micronutrients where sand rootzones are aggressively leached.

cool_season_fall_nitrogen.primary_goal: Maximize recovery, tillering, and carbohydrate storage during the best growing window for cool-season turf.
core_principles: Shift the largest annual nitrogen emphasis into fall when root growth and shoot recovery are more favorable.
Use fall fertility to repair summer thinning before winter rather than trying to rescue turf with late spring excess.
Coordinate fertility with overseeding, cultivation, and weed-control restrictions.

benchmark_ranges.split_program: 0.5-1.0 lb N/1000 sq ft total over multiple fall applications
benchmark_ranges.best_window: Early fall through mid-fall while growth is active
benchmark_ranges.late_fall_note: Late-fall N can be useful, but avoid creating soft growth immediately before severe cold

cautions: Do not over-apply soluble N where snow mold or lush late-season growth is a concern.
Respect seeding and herbicide timing conflicts.
Traffic-damaged areas may need cultivation and irrigation correction, not just more nitrogen.

warm_season_growth_season_nitrogen.primary_goal: Support bermudagrass and zoysiagrass density and recovery during active growth without excessive thatch or puffiness.
core_principles: Concentrate nitrogen during full active growth after green-up and before late-season slowdown.
Match rate to intensity of traffic, desired density, and clipping management.
Use less nitrogen where scalping, puffiness, or thatch are recurring problems.

benchmark_ranges.monthly_program: 0.25-0.5 lb N/1000 sq ft per month during active growth
benchmark_ranges.aggressive_recovery: Up to 0.75 lb N/1000 sq ft monthly when conditions and site goals justify it
benchmark_ranges.avoid_window: Do not push heavy nitrogen close to dormancy

cautions: Overfeeding can increase thatch, grain, and mowing pressure.
Late-season nitrogen may delay hardening before dormancy.
Zoysiagrass often needs less total N than bermudagrass for acceptable density.

sand_based_potassium_and_leaching.primary_goal: Reduce hidden hunger and stress sensitivity on sand-rootzone turf where nutrients move quickly.
suitable_sites: high-sand push-up greens with aggressive flushing
core_principles: Use soil and tissue testing together because sand profiles can test low yet respond differently depending on irrigation and growth.
Replace potassium more proactively on leached sites than on heavier native soils.
Balance potassium with total nitrogen demand instead of chasing a rigid ratio without context.

benchmark_ranges.application_style: Small repeated doses are usually safer than large corrective shots on sand
benchmark_ranges.testing_interval: Soil and/or tissue checks multiple times per season on high-input greens

cautions: Do not assume wilt equals potassium deficiency.
Localized dry spot and root damage can mimic low-potassium stress responses.
Excess salts from repeated fertility can worsen moisture stress if EC is ignored.

iron_color_vs_true_nitrogen_need.topic: Iron color response versus true nitrogen need
iron_color_vs_true_nitrogen_need.primary_goal: Separate cosmetic color management from actual growth and recovery needs.
core_principles: Use iron when the goal is color enhancement without a major growth increase.
Use nitrogen when the site needs real recovery, density, and new tissue production.
Interpret color, clipping yield, and stress tolerance together.

benchmark_ranges.decision_rule: Dark color with weak recovery still points to a growth limitation, not just a pigment issue

cautions: Repeated iron use can mask low nitrogen or root problems.
Do not substitute iron for rebuilding thin, traffic-damaged turf.
Tank mixes and low-pH sprays can increase burn risk under heat.

phosphorus_for_establishment_only.primary_goal: Use phosphorus strategically during establishment and rooting, not as a default maintenance crutch.
core_principles: Lean on soil testing and establishment status to decide phosphorus need.
Prioritize phosphorus when rooting into deficient soil or during establishment programs where labels and regulations allow it.
Move back to balanced maintenance fertility once turf is established unless tests justify otherwise.

benchmark_ranges.best_use_case: Seeding, sprigging, or low-testing soils with rooting demand

cautions: Avoid blanket phosphorus use where regulations restrict it.
Do not expect phosphorus alone to overcome compaction, poor seedbed prep, or weak irrigation coverage.
Documented low P matters more than tradition.
